enrichment.short_description: Open-source and cloud reference management platform for organizing, searching and AI-summarizing academic PDFs and citations.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (Open Source self-hosted plus paid cloud tiers)
enrichment.seo_title: I, Librarian Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: I, Librarian is an open-source reference manager with AI paper summaries and citation search. See its 2026 features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: I, Librarian is a reference and PDF management platform built for researchers, academic institutions and organizations that need to organize, search and analyze large collections of academic papers and documents, with a particular emphasis on privacy, data sovereignty and self-hosting. It is developed by Scilico, LLC, a Dallas, Texas-based software company that has focused on scientific and technical software since around 2015 and that maintains I, Librarian as both an open-source, self-hostable Community Edition and a commercially licensed cloud or self-hosted product. The platform works entirely through the browser, requiring no client software installation, and centers on a single, real-time, centralized repository of PDFs and references that a team or institution can search and share, rather than scattering references across individual researchers' local reference managers. Its feature set is built around integrated academic search across major scholarly and patent databases, letting users query and import records from sources including Crossref, Scopus, ScienceDirect, PubMed, PubMed Central, IEEE, NASA's database and a worldwide patent database exceeding 100 million records, alongside its own full-text and Boolean search across a user's stored library, aimed at supporting rigorous systematic literature reviews. More recent versions add AI-powered features: instant AI-generated summaries of uploaded papers, AI chat that lets researchers query across their entire document collection in natural language, and AI-generated text and note summaries within individual PDFs. Other capabilities include an in-browser PDF viewer with annotation tools, OCR for scanned documents, single sign-on authentication for institutional deployments, offline export of the app, audio notes, and activity logs for tracking usage across a team. I, Librarian is available in several tiers. A free, open-source Community Edition is distributed via GitHub for self-hosting at no cost, giving privacy-conscious users and smaller labs full control of their data without licensing fees. The commercial cloud product starts with a Lite plan at $2 per month base plus $1.50 per additional user and $0.50 per GB of storage, covering PDF organization, the in-browser viewer with annotations, full-text search, integrated search of Arxiv, Crossref, IEEE, NASA, PubMed and PubMed Central, OCR, SSO and email support. A Pro plan starts at $3 per month plus $2.50 per user and $0.50 per GB, adding AI chat and summaries, AI-powered literature discovery, integrated search of ScienceDirect, Scopus and the patent database, PDF text and note summaries, offline app export, audio notes and activity logs. For organizations that need on-premises deployment behind their own firewall, a Pro Self-Hosted license is available either as a $36 per year per user subscription or a $126 per user one-time perpetual license, delivered via container-based deployment with optional paid email support. Customers cited by the company include research-heavy organizations such as Imperial College London and biotech firms like Principia Biopharma, positioning I, Librarian's target audience as universities, healthcare and biotech companies, and engineering firms that need centralized, searchable, and often self-hosted reference libraries rather than individual desktop reference managers. It competes with Zotero, Mendeley, EndNote, JabRef and Papers, differentiating through its strong self-hosting and on-premises option for data-sovereignty-sensitive organizations, its centralized multi-user repository model, versus Zotero and Mendeley's more individual-library-centric design, and its built-in AI summarization and cross-library chat features layered directly into a self-hostable product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: I, Librarian is a reference and PDF management platform for researchers and institutions, developed by Scilico, LLC, a Dallas, Texas-based software company that has focused on scientific and technical software since around 2015.

It is available as a free, open-source, self-hostable Community Edition distributed on GitHub, and as a commercially licensed cloud or self-hosted product with additional AI-powered features, giving organizations a choice between a fully free option and a paid, more capable one.

The platform is built around a single, centralized, real-time repository of PDFs and references that a whole team or institution can search and share, an explicit alternative to scattering references across individual researchers' local reference managers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users can search across major scholarly and patent databases directly from within I, Librarian, including Crossref, Scopus, ScienceDirect, PubMed, PubMed Central, IEEE, NASA's database and a worldwide patent database exceeding 100 million records, alongside full-text and Boolean search of their own stored library.

Newer AI-powered features include instant AI-generated summaries of uploaded papers and AI chat that lets researchers query across their entire document collection in natural language, plus AI-generated text and note summaries within individual PDFs.

Other capabilities include an in-browser PDF viewer with annotation tools, OCR for scanned documents, single sign-on authentication for institutional deployments, offline app export, audio notes and activity logs for tracking team usage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free, open-source Community Edition can be self-hosted with no licensing cost, giving privacy-conscious teams full control of their data. The commercial cloud Lite plan starts at $2 per month plus $1.50 per additional user and $0.50 per GB of storage, while the Pro plan starts at $3 per month plus $2.50 per user and adds AI chat, AI summaries and access to premium databases like Scopus and ScienceDirect.

Organizations that need on-premises deployment behind their own firewall can choose a Pro Self-Hosted license, priced at $36 per year per user or $126 per user as a one-time perpetual license, delivered via container-based deployment with optional paid email support.

features.title: Centralized, real-time PDF repository
features.description: A single searchable library shared across a team or institution instead of individual desktop libraries.

features.title: Integrated academic and patent database search
features.description: Query Crossref, Scopus, ScienceDirect, PubMed, IEEE, NASA and a 100M+ patent database directly.

features.title: AI paper summaries and chat
features.description: Instantly summarize papers and query across an entire document collection in natural language.

features.title: In-browser PDF viewer with annotations
features.description: Read, highlight and annotate PDFs without leaving the browser.

features.title: OCR support
features.description: Extract searchable text from scanned documents.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment option
features.description: Run I, Librarian on-premises behind an organization's own firewall for full data control.

features.title: SSO authentication
features.description: Institutional single sign-on for managed, secure access.

features.title: Boolean and full-text search
features.description: Advanced search built for systematic literature reviews.

pros: Genuine open-source, self-hostable Community Edition gives full data ownership at no licensing cost.
Centralized, multi-user repository model suits institutional and team research settings better than single-user reference managers.
Broad integrated database search spanning academic literature and patents in one place.
Built-in AI summarization and cross-library chat are unusual among self-hostable reference managers.
Flexible pricing, per-user, per-GB, or one-time perpetual license, fits both small labs and larger institutions.
cons: Less mainstream and smaller community than Zotero or Mendeley, meaning fewer third-party integrations and browser plugins.
AI features and access to premium databases like Scopus and ScienceDirect are gated behind the Pro tier.
Self-hosted deployment requires technical setup and container management, unlike a fully managed SaaS.
Pricing structure combining a base fee, per-user cost and per-GB storage can be harder to estimate upfront than flat per-seat pricing.
Smaller company with a leaner support team than larger reference-management vendors.

faqs.question: Is I, Librarian open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free Community Edition is available on GitHub for self-hosting; commercial cloud and self-hosted Pro tiers add AI features and premium database access.

faqs.question: How much does I, Librarian cost?
faqs.answer: The cloud Lite plan starts at $2/month plus $1.50 per user and $0.50/GB storage; Pro starts at $3/month plus $2.50 per user; self-hosted Pro licenses run $36/year per user or $126 per user as a one-time perpetual license.

faqs.question: Who makes I, Librarian?
faqs.answer: I, Librarian is developed by Scilico, LLC, a Dallas, Texas-based software company.

faqs.question: Can I, Librarian search academic databases directly?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates search across Crossref, Scopus, ScienceDirect, PubMed, PubMed Central, IEEE, NASA and a large patent database.

faqs.question: Does I, Librarian have AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Pro tier includes AI-generated paper summaries and AI chat for querying across a user's entire document library.

faqs.question: Can I, Librarian be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, both a free open-source Community Edition and a paid Pro Self-Hosted license are available for on-premises deployment.
